stages.description: Stage 1 runs 20 km from Barcelona to Barcelona, climbing 220 m along the way (ProfileScore 23) — a team time trial, decided by collective pacing and team strength rather than one rider.

Stage 2 runs 168 km from Tarragona to Barcelona, climbing 2049 m along the way (ProfileScore 137) — a punchy, hilly finish that suits explosive one-day specialists over pure sprinters. Across the last four Tours, puncheur stages like this one were won from a breakaway 40% of the time (10 such stages). Riders who've won this type of stage before: Tadej Pogacar (2), Wout Van Aert (1), Simon Yates (1), Richard Carapaz (1).

Stage 3 runs 196 km from Granollers to Les Angles, climbing 3940 m along the way (ProfileScore 185) — a summit finish — the kind of stage that separates the GC contenders and pure climbers. Across the last four Tours, mtf stages like this one were won from a breakaway 40% of the time (20 such stages). picks.overnight_recap: Stage 2's Montjuïc puncheur finish produced the first shock of the race: Isaac del Toro (UAE) beat Pogačar in the reduced group sprint, with Evenepoel 3rd and Vingegaard 4th, all on Pogačar's own wheel — del Toro takes yellow and the white jersey outright, with Pogačar 2nd at +6s. It's the first time in four Tours Pogačar has lost a stage he was favourite for on a finish like this, and it opens a genuine, if early, UAE team-order question: does Pogačar ride for himself from here, or does del Toro get a run at leading his own team?
picks.stage_description: Granollers to Les Angles, 195.9 km, ~3,900 m climbing — the Tour's first proper mountain test, and its first summit finish (archetype mtf, ProfileScore 185). The route rolls through the Pyrenean foothills before the Col du Calvaire (1,836 m, the day's high point, cresting 23.6 km from the line), then a long descent into a genuinely steep uphill finish at the Les Angles ski station — the final 1.7 km ramping to 6.5%. Historically, 'mtf' stages in the last four Tours have gone to a breakaway ~40% of the time; when they haven't, Pogačar has personally won 8 of the other 12 (plus Vingegaard 3, and a handful of GC-adjacent or opportunist wins — Arensman, Poels, Paret-Peintre, Pidcock, Kwiatkowski, Woods). One flag: cyclingstage.com notes a possible route change is being discussed due to wildfire risk in the area — not yet confirmed as of tonight.
picks.expert_agreement_note: No expert has weighed in on Stage 3 specifically, or even reacted to Stage 2's del Toro upset yet — THEMOVE and Lanterne Rouge's latest uploads are still the pre-Tour preview and the Stage 1 TTT recap respectively (checked again tonight, nothing new posted). Tonight's read is model-led with zero expert corroboration either way — treat it accordingly.
picks.no_bet_reason: Close, but nothing clears the bar tonight. Pogačar (2.5, 27.6% de-vigged) is the obvious anchor — he's personally won 8 of the last 20 'mtf'-archetype stages in the last four Tours, an extraordinary base rate on exactly this terrain — but this market's overround (1.450) is sitting right at our max-trust ceiling, and our own honest probability estimate (~35-38%) only just brushes the 1.30x edge threshold rather than clearing it with room to spare. We're also not willing to treat Tuesday's puncheur loss as proof he's off — that was a reduced-group finish, not a real climbing test — but it does mean there's now a live, unresolved question of whether UAE rides for Pogačar or for their new race leader del Toro, and we won't guess team orders. Checked the obvious alternatives for a longshot instead: Van der Poel (12.0) isn't proven at this altitude/gradient combination; Vacek (13.0) and Grégoire (17.0) have no data-backed edge we can point to; Ben Healy (21.0) is the kind of rider who could animate a 40%-survival breakaway, but his actual 2026 season form is quiet (PCS rank 262 with 158 points, down hard from rank 22 last year) — not enough to back him on hope alone. Del Toro (19.0) is in the form of his life but genuinely too uncertain to price given the team-order question above. Nothing here is fabricated conviction — it's a night to watch, not bet.

Patrick Broe's Stage 1 TTT recap gives the key tactical detail for Stage 2: del Toro paced Pogačar on both the descent and into the final climb in the TTT, taking direct orders ('I need you to pace the descent... slingshot me into that final climb') — meaning del Toro is riding this Tour as UAE's lieutenant, not chasing his own stage wins, at least early on. Broe is unsure UAE fully commits to racing for the Stage 2 win itself: 'we got a punchy stage tomorrow, will Pogačar and UAE go for the win? I don't know.' Reads as tactically uncertain rather than a confident tip either way.

Armstrong and Bruyneel's pre-Tour preview frames Pogačar as the overwhelming favourite for the Tour and for any punchy finish like Montjuïc — the debate centres on whether anyone (Vingegaard, Evenepoel, or a resurgent Ineos) can consistently match him rather than beat him outright on a single stage. No stage-2-specific tip yet; daily stage previews begin with tonight's edition.

Structured stage-2 preview: 168.5km Tarragona-Barcelona finishing on the Montjuïc circuit, forecast 30-34C and sunny with a light tailwind on the coastal run before a high-pace finale on the circuit. Their framing: a punchy reduced-group finish rather than a bunch sprint, and the stage winner likely inherits the yellow jersey given how close the GC contenders sit after the TTT.

outrights.headline: No outright bets tonight. We had value on Mads Pedersen for green last night at 5.0 — TAB shortened him to 4.25 overnight and that edge is now gone in real terms (see Points below). GC, mountains, youth and teams remain efficiently priced or too short, most of them tighter than yesterday after Isaac del Toro's Stage 2 upset.

markets.name: Points classification
markets.summary: Pulling last night's Pedersen pick. Our probability estimate hasn't changed (~21%) but TAB shortened him from 5.0 to 4.25 overnight — at this market's overround (~1.50), the de-vig-ratio bar (1.30x) no longer guarantees a genuinely profitable price: 21% vs 4.25 works out to slightly negative expected value on the actual number offered (0.21 x 4.25 - 1 = -0.11), even though it still nominally clears the ratio threshold. When the ratio test and the real-price math disagree, the real price wins — no bet. Two non-sprint stages so far haven't told us anything new about the green race itself (no bunch sprint yet); this is purely a price move, not a form or model change. Worth re-checking once he shortens or lengthens again, or once a real sprint stage happens.

markets.name: General Classification
markets.summary: Pogačar's price barely moved despite losing Stage 2 to del Toro (1.28 -> 1.22) — the market still reads this as a minor blip in a 3-week race, and we agree: our estimate (~55-60%) sits close to the 55% de-vigged price now, so there's no longer even the faint gap we noted on day one. Vingegaard (4.4) is fairly priced as the genuine rival. Del Toro (17.0) is a fascinating story after 2 stages but far too speculative to price a 3-week GC outcome on. No value.

markets.name: Mountains classification
markets.summary: Barely changed from yesterday. Pogačar (2.25) remains favourite for the same reason as always — a dominant GC rider tends to collect summit points as a byproduct — but the price is efficient. Carapaz actually drifted slightly (4.75 -> 5.0); still fairly priced, not a value move. No confident bet; Carapaz remains the more interesting non-favourite angle if you want KOM exposure, but we're not calling it a bet at this price.

markets.name: Young rider classification
markets.summary: Del Toro backed up our read from yesterday — he now genuinely leads both GC and the white jersey after winning Stage 2 — but the market caught up with him: Seixas 2.25 -> 2.0 and del Toro 2.5 -> 2.25, both shorter, so there's no edge left to mine even though we called the right rider. Ayuso (5.0) is further back and unconvincing. Efficiently priced two-way race now; no bet.

markets.name: Teams classification
markets.summary: Any residual value here is gone. UAE tightened sharply overnight (1.85 -> 1.44, 55% de-vigged) after taking 1st and 2nd on GC through Stage 2 — the market has fully priced in exactly the depth advantage we flagged yesterday as a 'faint, too-thin' case. No bet, and no longer even a marginal one.
